tasks.title: Count Vowels in Each Word
tasks.description:
Strings are often processed one character at a time, especially when you need to classify letters or ignore punctuation. This is useful in text tools, search filters, and simple language processing, where a program must inspect every character and make decisions based on its type.

Read a line of text with at most 200 characters, which may contain spaces. Count how many vowels appear in the line, treating both uppercase and lowercase English vowels as vowels. Print the total count as a single integer followed by a newline. If the line is empty, print 0.

WORKED EXAMPLE:
Input: `Hello, World!` → count: 0 → 1 → 2 → 3. Output: `3`

tasks.title: Reverse Each Word in a Sentence
tasks.description:
Working with strings often means transforming text while preserving its structure. Reversing words is a common operation in text editing and formatting tasks, where each token must be processed without losing spaces between them.

Read one line of text with at most 200 characters, including spaces. Reverse the characters inside each word, but keep the words in the same order and keep every space exactly as it appears in the input. Print the transformed line followed by a newline. Words are maximal sequences of non-space characters.

WORKED EXAMPLE:
Input: `ab cd ef` → output text changes from `ba cd fe`. Output: `ba cd fe`

tasks.title: First Repeated Character
tasks.description:
Searching through strings is a basic form of pattern detection. Programs that validate input, detect anomalies, or compress data often need to remember what has been seen before while scanning from left to right.

Read a line of text with at most 200 characters, including spaces. Find the first character that appears at least twice when scanning from left to right, and print that character followed by a newline. If no character repeats, print `NONE`. Spaces are valid characters and may be the repeated character.

WORKED EXAMPLE:
Input: `abca` → seen sequence: `a → b → c → a` and the first repeat is `a`. Output: `a`

tasks.title: Compress Consecutive Letters
tasks.description:
String compression is useful when repeated characters appear in logs, codes, or simple data storage. A run-length style representation replaces long stretches of the same symbol with a shorter description, which makes repeated patterns easier to summarize.

Read one line of text with at most 200 characters, including spaces. Compress each maximal block of identical characters into the character followed immediately by the number of times it appears in that block. Print the compressed result followed by a newline. If the input line is empty, print nothing except the newline.

WORKED EXAMPLE:
Input: `aaabbc` → blocks become `a3 → b2 → c1`. Output: `a3b2c1`

tasks.title: Palindrome Ignoring Spaces and Case
tasks.description:
Palindrome checks are common in text validation and word games. A palindrome is a string that reads the same forward and backward, and many applications need to compare text while ignoring formatting differences such as capitalization and spaces.

Read one line of text with at most 200 characters. Consider only alphabetic letters, ignore spaces and case, and determine whether the resulting sequence is a palindrome. Print `YES` if it is, otherwise print `NO`, followed by a newline. An empty filtered sequence should be considered a palindrome.

WORKED EXAMPLE:
Input: `A man a plan a canal Panama` → filtered comparison moves inward and matches throughout. Output: `YES`

test_cases.input: A man a plan a canal Panama

test_results.input: A man a plan a canal Panama

tasks.title: Count Words Starting and Ending with a Vowel
tasks.description:
Text scanners often need to analyze words individually rather than as a whole line. This matters in document processing and simple search tools, where letters at the beginning and end of each word can influence formatting, filtering, or statistics.

Read one line of text with at most 200 characters, including spaces. Count how many words start and end with a vowel, where vowels are `a`, `e`, `i`, `o`, `u` in either case. Words are maximal sequences of non-space characters. Print the count followed by a newline. If there are no words, print 0.

WORKED EXAMPLE:
Input: `apple ice orange` → word checks lead to counts of 1 → 2 → 3. Output: `3`

tasks.title: Remove Duplicate Characters, Keep First Occurrence
tasks.description:
Deduplication is a common string-processing task in data cleaning and user-input handling. When text contains repeated letters or symbols, programs may need to keep only the first appearance of each character while preserving the original order.

Read one line of text with at most 200 characters, including spaces. Print a new line containing each distinct character from the input exactly once, in the order of its first appearance. Spaces are treated like ordinary characters and may appear in the output at most once. Finish with a newline. If the input line is empty, print just a newline.

WORKED EXAMPLE:
Input: `banana` → output builds as `b → ba → ban`. Output: `ban`

tasks.title: Shift Letters by a Given Amount
tasks.description:
Character-by-character transformation is used in simple encoding, scrambling, and formatting tasks. When letters are shifted within the alphabet, the program must preserve the difference between letters and non-letters while handling wrap-around correctly.

Read an integer `k` followed by one line of text with at most 200 characters. Shift every English letter forward by `k` positions in the alphabet, wrapping around after `z` or `Z`. Preserve case and leave all non-letter characters unchanged. The integer `k` may be any value from 0 to 1000. Print the transformed line followed by a newline.

WORKED EXAMPLE:
Input: `2 abc XYZ` → letters change to `cde ZAB`. Output: `cde ZAB`

tasks.title: Longest Word in a Sentence
tasks.description:
Finding the longest word is a simple form of text analysis used in document statistics and input validation. To do it correctly, a program must separate words, compare their lengths, and remember the best candidate seen so far.

Read one line of text with at most 200 characters, including spaces. Words are maximal sequences of non-space characters. Print the longest word in the line; if several words have the same maximum length, print the one that appears first. If the line contains no words, print `NONE`. End with a newline.

WORKED EXAMPLE:
Input: `one three seven` → lengths compare as `3 → 5 → 5`, so the first longest word remains. Output: `three`

tasks.title: Count Occurrences of a Pattern in a String
tasks.description:
Pattern matching is a core idea in text processing, from searching documents to analyzing DNA-like symbol sequences. Even a simple count of matches requires examining neighboring characters repeatedly and handling overlapping occurrences carefully.

Read two lines: the first line contains a text string with at most 200 characters, and the second line contains a non-empty pattern with at most 200 characters. Count how many times the pattern appears in the text, including overlapping matches. Print the count followed by a newline. If the pattern is longer than the text, the answer is 0.

WORKED EXAMPLE:
Input: `aaaa` and `aa` → matches occur starting at positions 1, 2, and 3. Output: `3`
